coredata.dc:title: Role of leachate characteristics on carbon dots/TiO2 photocatalytic and bactericidal inactivation mechanism
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Qian
Wang, Tong
Li, Huan
Xu, Qiyong
coredata.dc:description: Photocatalytic processes, recognized for their environmental compatibility, have gained increasing attention in leachate treatment. The photocatalytic performance is associated with leachate characteristics and microbial community. However, the influence and mechanism of leachate characteristics and microbial community on the photocatalytic performance remains poorly understood. To address the knowledge gap, we compared the photocatalytic performance of carbon dots/P25 (CDs/P25) in four kinds of landfill leachate. The CDs/P25 could effectively kill bacteria in different leachates through deforming bacterial membranes. After the CDs/P25 photocatalytic process, the percentage of dead bacteria increased to over 80.0 % in different leachates. Moreover, the CDs/P25 could change the signal molecules and reshape the microbial communities, and the Bacteroidota phylum bacteria were killed, thus inhibiting the formation of biofilm. After photocatalytic process, the proportion of quorum sensing declined from 61.6 % to 55.5 % in leachate C. The redundancy analysis indicated that the pH (40.8 %) was the predominant factor for the photocatalytic performance of the CDs/P25 in leachate. These findings not only gain a deeper understanding of bacterial inactivation of the CDs/P25 photocatalytic process in leachate, but also provide a scientific basis for the effective application of the CDs/P25 photocatalysis in leachate treatment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Landfill leachate
Photocatalytic
Carbon dots
Microbial community
Metagenomics

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Relationships between photocatalytic performance and leachate characteristics

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Photocatalytic degradation of different leachate characteristics. (a) pH; (b) COD; (c) DOC; (d) NH4 +-N; (e) UV254; (f) VIS400.

3D-EEM images of different leachate before and after degradation by photocatalytic process (a) leachate A0; (b) leachate B0; (c) leachate C0; (d) leachate A4; (e) leachate B4; (f) leachate C4; (g) leachate D0; (h) leachate D4. (i) Composition distribution in different leachate.

SEM images of different leachate groups. (a) leachate A0; (b) leachate B0; (c) leachate C0; (d) leachate D0; (e) leachate A4; (f) leachate B4; (g) leachate C4; (h) leachate D4. Red arrows indicate the presence of bacterial cells.
(a) CLSM images of bacteria in different leachate groups. (b) distribution of live cells, dead cells, and EPS. (c) bacteria quantities in different leachate groups.

Microbial community analysis. a) rank-abundance curve at OTU level. b-1) Venn figure at OTU level. b-2) total OTU (dark color) and specific OTU (light color). c) microbial diversity at phylum level. d) microbial diversity at class level.

a) Distribution of signal molecules in different leachate samples. b) Relative abundance of quorum sensing bacteria at the phylum level. c) Relative abundance of quorum quenching bacteria at the phylum level.
a) Relative abundance of top 15 functional pathways predicted and counted from KEGG database. b) Relative abundance of biofilm formation functional pathways in different leachate samples.

a) Pearson’s correlation analysis between photocatalytic performance and leachate characteristics. b) Redundancy analysis of the relationship between photocatalytic performance and leachate characteristics.
Characteristics of different leachate used in this study.

head.ce:title: Role of leachate characteristics on carbon dots/TiO2 photocatalytic and bactericidal inactivation mechanism

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shenzhen International Graduate School, Tsinghua University, Shenzhen, 518055, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photocatalytic processes, recognized for their environmental compatibility, have gained increasing attention in leachate treatment. The photocatalytic performance is associated with leachate characteristics and microbial community. However, the influence and mechanism of leachate characteristics and microbial community on the photocatalytic performance remains poorly understood. To address the knowledge gap, we compared the photocatalytic performance of carbon dots/P25 (CDs/P25) in four kinds of landfill leachate. The CDs/P25 could effectively kill bacteria in different leachates through deforming bacterial membranes. After the CDs/P25 photocatalytic process, the percentage of dead bacteria increased to over 80.0 % in different leachates. Moreover, the CDs/P25 could change the signal molecules and reshape the microbial communities, and the Bacteroidota phylum bacteria were killed, thus inhibiting the formation of biofilm. After photocatalytic process, the proportion of quorum sensing declined from 61.6 % to 55.5 % in leachate C. The redundancy analysis indicated that the pH (40.8 %) was the predominant factor for the photocatalytic performance of the CDs/P25 in leachate. These findings not only gain a deeper understanding of bacterial inactivation of the CDs/P25 photocatalytic process in leachate, but also provide a scientific basis for the effective application of the CDs/P25 photocatalysis in leachate treatment.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Landfill leachate treatment poses a critical challenge in global municipal solid waste (MSW) management [1]. It was reported that billions of tons of leachate are generated annually worldwide [2]. Landfill leachate is a heterogeneous mixture consisting of percolated rainwater, water produced by the biodegradation of waste, and the inherent water within the waste itself [3,4]. Due to the heterogeneity and decomposition of MSW, leachate contains complex and various organics and inorganics, including dissolved organic matter, inorganic salts, heavy metals, and xenobiotic organic compounds [5]. Meanwhile, the highly diverse microbial community was also observed in leachate, interacting with organic contaminants, changing microbial metabolic functions, and affecting the leachate characteristics [6]. Thus, there is a need to treat leachate effectively and appropriately to prevent ecotoxicity and environmental damage.

Many technologies have been developed for leachate treatment, including biological, physical, chemical, and other processes [5]. Recently, photocatalytic degradation has emerged as an innovative chemical method for leachate treatment due to its non-toxic, eco-friendliness, and chemical stability [7–9]. Previous studies have demonstrated that the photocatalytic process could effectively degrade organic pollutants and kill bacteria in leachate [10,11]. For instance, Lalavi et al. [12] reported that boron-doped TiO2 photocatalytic process could remove 58.6 % of chemical oxygen demand (COD) in young landfill leachate. Li et al. [13] reported that the Au/Ag-TiO2 membrane could decrease the bacterial viability of Escherichia coli to 1.5 % after photocatalysis. It is well known that the bacterial communities can vary and interact with the leachate characteristics, such as pH, electrical conductivity, and ammonia nitrogen [6,14]. However, to the best of the authors’ knowledge, studies on the influence and mechanism of leachate characteristics and microbial community on photocatalytic performance have not been reported.

In general, a photocatalyst is loaded on the surface of a carrier [15]. Bacterial growth and biofilm formation on the carrier would limit the photocatalytic performance due to its block effect between photocatalyst and light. Our previous study showed that incorporating carbon dots and TiO2 could remove 83.3 % bacteria in old landfill leachate [16]. However, bacterial species can communicate with each other in the complex bacterial community through quorum sensing (QS) using signal substances, thus regulating bacterial community and biofilm formation [17,18]. Collectively, the photocatalytic performance is associated with microbial diversity and function, as well as leachate characteristics. Therefore, it is necessary to elucidate the interacting influence between the microbial community and photocatalytic performance in leachate.

In this study, we aimed to elucidate the role of leachate characteristics on the CDs/P25 photocatalytic performance in leachate and clarify the mechanism of bacterial inactivation from a metagenomic perspective. The CDs/P25 photocatalyst was applied in four kinds of bottom ash co-disposal landfill leachates. Then, the microbial community and metagenomics analysis were used to elucidate the mechanism of bacterial inactivation. Moreover, the relationship between leachate characteristics and photocatalytic performance (pollutant degradation and bacterial inactivation) was evaluated in leachate. The findings of this study shed light on the role of leachate characteristics on the CDs/P25 photocatalytic processes and offered novel perspectives for optimizing the application of photocatalytic processes in leachate treatment.

Leachate was collected from four kinds of laboratory-scale simulated landfills as described in our previous references [19]. Four kinds of landfills were divided according to the mass ratio of municipal solid waste and bottom ash, including 1:0, 9:1, 1:1, and 0:1, abbreviated as leachate A, leachate B, leachate C, leachate D, respectively. All reactors were operated under anaerobic conditions at 35 ± 2℃ for 4.5 years. The characteristics of different leachates are listed in Table 1.

Carbon dots- TiO2 P25 (CDs/P25) was prepared according to our previous study [16]. Typically, a homogeneous dispersion of 2.52 g citric acid monohydrate in 60 mL ultrapure water was obtained by using magnetic stirring at adjusting pH= 6. Subsequently, 0.96 g of TiO2-P25 was added to the solution assisted by ultrasonication for 30 min. The mixture was then transferred to a hydrothermal Teflon autoclave and heated at 160℃ for 4 h. Finally, the sediments were collected via centrifugation, followed by washing with ethanol and ultrapure water, and dried at 60℃ for 12 h.

The photocatalytic experiments were conducted in a reactor using a Xenon lamp (300 W, ceaulight, CEL-HXF). The distance between the light source and the sample was within 12 cm based on our previous study [16]. Before the photoreaction, 125 mg of the CDs/P25 was added to the leachate solution and stirred for 60 min in darkness to achieve adsorption-desorption equilibrium. During the photocatalytic degradation reaction, 5 mL of dispersed solution was collected at 30 min time intervals. The whole photocatalytic process lasted for 240 min. In this study, A0, B0, C0, and D0 were used to represent the original leachate samples before the photocatalytic process, while A4, B4, C4, and D4 were used to represent the leachate samples obtained after the whole photocatalytic process.

For the leachate degradation activity, the chemical oxygen demand (COD), dissolved organic carbon (DOC), ammonia, UV254, and VIS400 were conducted. The COD and ammonia were detected by a water quality analyzer (7500, Palintest, England). The DOC was analyzed by a TOC analyzer (Shimadzu, TOC-L CSN). The UV254 and VIS400 were detected to show the degradation rates of humic acid and fulvic acid, respectively. The pH was measured by a portable meter (Five easy plus, Mettler Toledo, Switzerland). The excitation-emission matrix (EEM) spectra of various samples were acquired using a fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi, F-7000). The emission wavelengths (Em) were ranging from 280 to 560 nm, while the excitation wavelengths (Ex) were ranging from 220 to 420 nm with increments of 5 nm and 1 nm, respectively.

For the bacteria disinfection activity, metagenomic sequencing, 16 S rRNA sequencing analysis, quantitative PCR, and bacterial cell viability were conducted. For metagenomic sequencing, 16 S rRNA sequencing analysis and quantitative PCR, 338 F (5′-ACTCCTACGGGAGGCAGCAG-3′)/806 R (5′-GGACTACHVGGGTWTCTAAT-3′) bacterial primer set were used. The Kyoto Encyclopedia of Genes and Genomes (KEGG) database was performed for the function and metabolic pathways of gene annotation via the BLASTP program with an E-value cutoff of 10−5. The metagenomic sequencing was analyzed based on the method of our previous study [18]. The proportions of seven QS signal molecules were calculated according to a previous study [20], including acyl-homoserine lactone (AHL), autoinducing peptides (AIP), autoinducing peptides (AI-2), inter- and intra-species diffusible signal factor (DSF), second messenger cyclic dimeric guanosine monophosphate (c-di-GMP), quinolone-like 2-heptyl-3-hydroxy-4-quinolone (PQS), and others. The QS signal molecules related gene numbers were identified from selected KEGG Orthologs (KOs) in the KEGG database. The proportion of each signaling molecule is calculated as the ratio of the number of genes associated with that signaling molecule to the total number of genes in the sample.

The bacterial cell viability was performed by confocal laser scanning microscopy (A1 R, Nikon, Japan) with two dyes, including SYTO9 and PI for live and dead bacteria, respectively. The bacterial morphology was analyzed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (Supra®55, Lecia, Germany) at 15 kV. The SEM samples were prepared based on a previous study [21].

The results were shown as mean ± standard deviation. The Pearson’s correlation between leachate characteristics and photocatalytic effects was calculated and compared using the PASW software (SPSS Inc., USA). Due to the complexity and interaction of different leachate characteristics, the redundancy analysis (RDA) was further conducted to evaluate the relationship between the leachate characteristics and photocatalytic effects using Canoco (Version 5.02, ScientiaPro, Hungary).

The functional groups were compared in the CDs/P25 and P25 (Fig. S1). The bands at 1630 and 3440 cm−1 were observed in the CDs/P25 and P25, representing for the bending and stretching vibration of the surface hydroxyl (O-H) group, respectively [22]. However, three new bands at 1400, 1450, and 2920 cm−1 were detected in the CDs/P25, which were attributed to C-O-C, C-N = , and C-H, respectively [23,24]. These results suggested that the successful coupling of CDs and TiO2 in the CDs/P25.

As illustrated in Fig. 1, the photocatalytic degradation performance of leachate was varied in four kinds of leachate. The pH values in leachate A, B, and C were slightly increased during the photocatalytic process, while the pH values in leachate D were mildly decreased (Fig. 1a). These results showed the pH values were stable in the photocatalytic process, indicating that the CDs/P25 was stable. After 240 minutes of photocatalysis, the COD degradation rates in leachates A, B, C, and D were 68.4 %, 56.4 %, 38.2 %, and 35.6 %, respectively (Fig. 1b). In contrast, the DOC degradation rates exhibited a different trend: leachate C had the highest rate at 34.7 %, followed by leachate D (27.7 %), leachate A (27.6 %), and leachate B (13.3 %) (Fig. 1c). Notably, the DOC degradation rates were generally lower than the corresponding COD degradation rates within the same leachate type. For instance, in leachate C, the DOC degradation rate (34.7 %) was lower than the COD degradation rate (38.2 %). A previous study has reported that the Ag-TiO2 photocatalytic process could achieve maximum COD and DOC degradation rates of 62 % and 55 %, respectively [25]. Similar trends have been observed in other studies [26,27].

As shown in Fig. 1d, the ammonia nitrogen degradation rates in leachates A, B, C, and D were 10.3 %, 38.5 %, 17.2 %, and 31.9 %, respectively. These results suggested that the CDs/P25 photocatalytic process could effectively degrades ammonia nitrogen across various leachates. The UV254 and VIS400 removal efficiency in the leachate A were 68.8 % and 97.9 %, while the UV254 and VIS400 degradation efficiency in the leachate C were 68.3 % and 89.1 %, respectively (Fig. 1e, f). Overall, these results indicated that the CDs/P25 photocatalysis were effective in treating leachate organic matters. It was reported that boron-doped TiO2 could degrade 58.6 % COD after 12 h photocatalysis [12]. Becerra et al. [28] found that the P25 can degrade 68 % COD and 76 % DOC after 4.3 h irradiation. It was noted that the CDs/P25 was capable of degrading organics matters in leachate according to the literature [8].

To better illustrate the photocatalytic effect on organic matters, the percentages of certain organic matters were compared during the photocatalytic process ( Fig. 2). Before the photocatalytic process, four kinds of leachate were rich in the regions Ⅱ, Ⅲ, Ⅳ, and Ⅴ, indicating that high pollutant concentration. Five components were identified, including tyrosine-like compounds (region I), tryptophan-like compounds (region II), fulvic acids (region Ⅲ), soluble microbial products (region Ⅳ), and humic acid (region Ⅴ) [29]. After the photocatalytic process, the percentages of humic acid (region Ⅴ) in the leachate A decreased from 18.7 % (A0) to 9.7 % (A4). Conversely, the percentages of soluble microbial products (region Ⅳ) in the leachate A increased from 16.4 % (A0) to 38.4 % (A4). Similar trends were observed in the leachate B and C. To sum up, after photocatalysis, the percentages of tryptophan-like compounds, fulvic acids, and humic acids in the leachate decreased, while the percentages of tyrosine-like compounds and soluble microbial products increased. These results suggested that organic matters in the leachate were destructed into smaller, less aromatic molecules.

A blue shift in the EEM spectrum was observed in all leachate after photocatalysis, indicating a decrease in number of aromatic rings condensed in straight chains, conjugated double bonds and formational changes. The decrease in fluorescence intensity was attributed to the destruction of humic acid, including the increase of the carboxylic functional group and the relative carboxylic content [30]. Overall, the CDs/P25 could effectively degrade leachate in varying degrees.

The morphology of the leachate components and the CDs/P25 catalysts were characterized by SEM ( Fig. 3). Dispersive agglomerated CDs/P25 catalysts were observed in all leachate samples, conversely, the morphology of the bacteria was varied in different leachate. For instance, various rod-like and ball-like bacteria were observed in the leachate A0 (Fig. 3a), however, few bacteria were shown in the leachate B0, C0, and D0 (Fig. 3b-d). These bacterial differences were attributed to the leachate characteristics in leachate samples. It was reported that the microbial community structure of leachate was significantly correlated with leachate characteristics, such as ammonia nitrogen, and conductivity [6]. Moreover, the size and shape of bacteria changed after the CDs/P25 photocatalysis, and the bacterial membranes became deformed and collapsed, indicating the leakage of intracellular substances by substantial degradation of the bacterial membranes (Fig. 3e-h). These results suggested the CDs/P25 could effectively kill or degrade bacteria. It was reported that TiO2 could inhibit or kill bacteria by adsorbing TiO2 on the surface of bacteria [31]. The addition of CDs could degrade more microorganisms and cause oxidative damage [32]. It was noted that our previous study showed no biofilm was formed in the geotextile of leachate D [19], however, in this study, few bacteria were observed in leachate D, which was mixed by the CDs/P25 photocatalyst (Fig. 3d). This difference was assumed to the direct contact between bacteria and CDs/P25 photocatalyst in leachate, which may increase the bactericidal effect. Previous studies have demonstrated that powder TiO2 photocatalysts have a large surface area and pore size, enabling effective interaction with bacterial cells and promoting strong adhesion to and accumulation on bacterial surfaces [33,34]. The surface area of CDs/P25 was larger than that of commercial TiO2 (P25) [16,35]. Therefore, it can be inferred that CDs/P25 has a higher capacity for adsorbing bacteria in leachates. It was reported that direct contact between bacteria cells and photocatalysts was essential for inducing membrane damage and generating reactive species that lead to bacterial inactivation [34,36].

As shown in Fig. 4, the distribution of live bacteria, dead bacteria, and EPS was characterized in different leachates. Green, red, and purple represent live bacteria, dead bacteria, and EPS, respectively. Leachate A0, B0, and C0 were mainly composed of live bacteria, accounting for 97.3 ± 0.1 %, 68.0 ± 0.4 %, and 88.8 ± 2.2 %, respectively. The staining was not obvious in the leachate D0, indicating that few biofilm occurred in the leachate D0, which was in accordance with the SEM images (Fig. 3). After photocatalysis, the percentage of live bacteria decreased sharply, while the percentage of dead bacteria increased. The percentage of dead bacteria in the leachate A4, B4, and C4 was 96.8 ± 0.1 %, 80.0 ± 2.8 %, and 97.9 ± 0.6 %, respectively. The dead bacteria dye PI reagent can only combine with DNA in the broken bacterial membrane [37]. These results suggested that the CDs/P25 photocatalyst could kill bacteria through deformed bacterial membrane. However, no obvious presence and change was observed in the percentage of EPS in all leachate samples, indicating that the EPS was not the main component of the biofilm and the CDs/P25 photocatalysis did not affect the presence of EPS. As illustrated in Fig. 4c, the bacterial gene copies in the leachate A0 (2.73 ×109 ± 0.02 ×109 copies/mL) were the highest, followed by the leachate B0 (1.95 ×109 ± 0.05 ×109 copies/mL). The amount of the leachate C0 and D0 was two or three orders of magnitude lower than the leachate A0 and B0. After photocatalysis, the bacterial gene copies decreased sharply. For instance, the degradation percentage of the leachate A and leachate B was 86.8 % and 89.7 %, respectively. Above all, the CDs/P25 could effectively kill bacteria by destroying the membrane.

The species abundance and distribution evenness of microbial communities in different leachates were analyzed ( Fig. 5a). On the horizontal axis, the distribution ranges in the leachate B0, A0, C0, and D0 were wider than those of B4, A4, C4, and D4. On the vertical axis, the curves in the leachate B4, A4, C4, and D4 were flatter than those of B0, A0, C0, and D0. It was reported that the wider the curve width, the greater the species composition abundance. The smoother curve, the more even distribution [38]. Thus, the CDs/P25 photocatalytic process could effectively decrease the microbial richness and evenness. Similar microbial effects have been reported with other photocatalyst (C3N4) [39]. As shown in Figs. 5b-1, the microbial communities pose common OTU species, and every leachate group contained unique OTU species. The common OTUs in different leachate samples was 123, while the unique OTUs in the leachate A, B, C, and D were 580, 464, 368, and 53, respectively (Fig. 5b-2). The ratios of the unique OTUs accounting for the total OTUs were 56.3 %, 46.8 %, 44.7 %, and 16.9 %, respectively. The leachate D (314) contained the lowest total OTUs species among these leachates, which was in consistent with the SEM, CLSM, and Q-PCR results. Thus, the microbial community in the leachate D could not be analyzed metagenomically, which was in line with our previous study [19].

Fig. 5c illustrates the top 10 in the relative abundance of the microbial community in different leachate samples at the phylum level. The results showed that the main phyla were Proteobacteria, Firmicutes, and Bacteroidota for all leachates, accounting for 98.5 %. However, the composition of bacterial phyla in different leachate samples showed a clear difference. the percentage of Proteobacteria in the leachate D (80 %) was the highest among different leachate samples, while the Chloroflexi was only detected in the leachate B groups. At the class level, the percentage of Campylobacteria in the leachate A groups was 20 %, which was higher than other leachate samples. The leachate D samples mainly contained Gammaproteobacteria and Alphaproteobacteria, accounting for over 85 % (Fig. 5d). Moreover, after the CDs/P25 photocatalysis, the percentage of Bacteroidota decreased in all leachates. In contrast, the percentage of Proteobacteria increased in the leachate A, B, and D, while it decreased in leachate C. The percentage of Proteobacteria in the leachate C0 and C4 was 68.0 % and 55.1 %, respectively. These results suggested that photocatalysis had a less pronounced effect on Proteobacteria compared to Bacteroidota. This observation aligns with a previous study indicating that Proteobacteria exhibit greater adaptability to environmental changes due to their diverse species composition [40]. The Bacteroidota were reported to be involved in the initial stage and formation of biofilm [41]. Above all, these microbial community results indicated that the CDs/P25 photocatalytic process could kill the Bacteroidota, such as Gammaproteobacteria and Alphaproteobacteria, thus inhibiting the formation of biofilm through reducing the bacterial adhesion.

As summarized in Fig. 6a), seven subgroups of signal molecules were detected and compared in different leachate groups. The c-di-GMP, AHL, and AIP systems were the main subgroups of signal molecules, while AI-2 and DSF were at a relatively lower level. Among these signal molecules, the AHL was regarded as the valuable clue that coordinated the behavior of the whole bacterial community [20,42]. It was reported that the AHL could expand the regulatory networks in different bacteria even at a small percentage compared to other signaling molecules [43]. Besides, as Fig. 5 discussed, the main phyla Proteobacteria and Bacteroidota belong to gram-negative bacteria, which were communicated through the AHL signal system [44]. Thus, we focused on the AHL signal in this study. After the CDs/P25 photocatalytic process, the percentage of AHL in leachate A remained nearly constant, ranging from 0.052 % to 0.054 %. Conversely, a decreasing trend of the percentage of AHL was observed in the leachate B and C. These results implied that the CDs/P25 photocatalysis affected the distribution of AHL signal molecules in different trends due to the leachate characteristics.

Further, the AHL related microbial community were analyzed in different leachate samples (Fig. 6b-c). As shown in Fig. 6b, the CDs/P25 photocatalysis changed the distribution of microbial communities related to AHL quorum sensing. The percentage of Proteobacteria decreased in the leachate A from 67.9 % to 62.8 %, while the percentage of Firmicutes increased in the leachate A from 14.7 % to 28.7 %. In the leachate B group, the percentage of Proteobacteria and Chloroflexi increased by 5.0 % and 11.7 %, respectively. The percentage of Firmicutes and Others decreased by 1.6 % and 12.3 %, respectively. In the leachate C group, the percentage of Proteobacteria and Firmicutes showed a similar trend with the leachate A group. In addition, the distribution of microbial communities related to AHL quorum quenching changed accordingly after the CDs/P25 photocatalysis. An increasing trend was observed in the percentage of Proteobacteria in all leachate groups, along with a decreasing trend in the percentage of Bacteroidota and Firmicutes. For instance, the percentage of Proteobacteria in the leachate A group increased from 66.4 % to 84.4 %, whereas the percentage of Bacteroidota in the leachate B group decreased from 4.2 % to 1.8 %. Overall, these results suggested that the CDs/P25 photocatalysis could change the AHL signal, thus the structure of microbial communities reshaped and influence the biofilm formation.

To better understand the photocatalytic effect on microbial metabolism, the top 15 functional pathways were compared in different leachate samples ( Fig. 7a). The metabolic pathways, biosynthesis of secondary metabolites, microbial metabolism in diverse environments, biosynthesis of cofactors, biosynthesis of amino acids, and carbon metabolism were part of the metabolism group, which was the highest proportion group in all leachate samples, accounting for over 16 %. Specifically, the quorum sensing (1.9 %, 1.8 %, and 1.9 % for the LA0, LB0, and LC0, respectively) was the main pathway in the group of cellular processes, which was reported to be associated with the biofilm formation in microbial communities [45].

As illustrated in Fig. 7b, the CDs/P25 photocatalytic process could inhibit the biofilm formation in different leachate groups to varying degrees. After photocatalytic process, the percentage of biofilm formation of Vibrio cholerae decreased from 12.1 % in the LA0 to 10.8 % in the LA4. The percentage of biofilm formation of Escherichia coli in the LB0 and LB4 was 11.7 % and 10.9 %, respectively. It was noted that the proportion of quorum sensing declined from 61.6 % in the LC0 to 55.5 % in the LC4. These results indicated that the CDs/P25 photocatalytic process showed a negative regulation of the biofilm formation. In addition, previous studies have demonstrated that the CDs/P25 photocatalytic process can generate reactive oxygen species (ROS), including hydroxyl radical (•OH), superoxide anion (•O2 −), and singlet oxygen (1O2) [16,23]. It was reported that •OH and •O2 − were the primary extracellular ROS responsible for oxidative damage to the bacterial cells and biofilm inactivation during TiO2 photocatalysis [46]. Therefore, it can be inferred that the CDs/P25 photocatalytic process generated ROS, which attacked bacterial cells, leading to membrane deformation and subsequent cellular dysfunction, as well as biofilm inactivation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Relationships between photocatalytic performance and leachate characteristics
ce:section.ce:para: As illustrated in Fig. 8a, Pearson’s linear correlation analysis was applied to explore the relationship between photocatalytic performance and leachate characteristics. Conflicting effects were observed between the photocatalytic performance and cation concentration in leachate. The COD degradation rate was negatively correlated with Na+ (R=-0.95, p ≤ 0.01) and K+ (R=-0.97, p ≤ 0.01), respectively. In contrast, the bacterial quantities reduction rate was positively correlated with Mg2+ (R=0.91, p ≤ 0.01). It was reported that the pollutant and catalyst types were the determining factors in the influence of photocatalytic performance [47]. Moreover, the live cell reduction rate was positively correlated with the UV254 degradation rate (R=0.95, p ≤ 0.01) and VIS400 degradation rate (R=0.92, p ≤ 0.01), respectively. UV254 and VIS400 could reflect the concentration of organic matter in the leachate [48]. Specifically, UV254 is used to indicate aromatic and refractory compounds [49], whereas VIS400 is regarded as color-forming moieties [50]. These results indicated that the CDs photocatalytic process could simultaneously degrade the organic matter and kill bacteria in different leachates to varying degrees.

Moreover, redundancy analysis was used to show the relationship between photocatalytic performance and leachate characteristics (Fig. 8b). The RDA1 and RDA2 accounted for 59.28 % and 26.48 % of the photocatalytic performance of different leachates, respectively. The two axes accounted for 85.76 % of the photocatalytic performance, indicating that the leachate characteristics considerably influenced the photocatalytic performance. The pH (40.8 %), conductivity (40.3 %), and Mg2+ (14.4 %) could primarily explain the difference in photocatalytic performance (Table S1). These results suggested that pH and inorganic ions were the dominant factors of the CDs/P25 photocatalytic performance, consistent with the Pearson’s correlation analysis. It was reported that solution pH could influence the catalyst surface area, the contaminant properties, and the reaction kinetics [51]. In addition, the inorganic ions in leachate could act as radical scavengers, influencing the contaminant transport to the catalyst surface, thus influence the photocatalytic performance [52]. As mentioned in Section 3.2, the CDs/P25 photocatalysis kills the bacteria by disrupting the integrity of the bacterial membrane. Once the pH and inorganic ions in the leachate changed, the bacterial community in the leachate would change accordingly. Meanwhile, the interaction between the bacteria and the surface of photocatalysts would change, thus predetermining the bacteria disinfection effect [15]. Overall, the pH and inorganic ions were the predominant factors in the CDs/P25 photocatalytic performance of the leachate, including organic matter degradation and bacteria disinfection.

In this study, the influence of leachate characteristics on the CDs/P25 photocatalytic performance was systematically elucidated. Organic matter degradation and bacterial inactivation were comprehensively characterized, while the microbial community structure, microbial genomics, and functional pathways were progressively revealed. These findings enhance our fundamental understanding of the CDs/P25 photocatalytic process in leachate and promote its practical application.

Indeed, leachate contains a complex and diverse array of organic and inorganic compounds, including dissolved organic matter, inorganic salts, heavy metals, and xenobiotic organic compounds [5]. Our findings demonstrated that the CDs/P25 photocatalytic process can effectively degrade organic matter in leachate. For instance, after the CDs/P25 photocatalytic process, the COD degradation rate was in the range of 35.6–68.4 %. However, the influence of the CDs/P25 photocatalytic process on the heavy metal and xenobiotic organic compounds in leachate should be further investigated in the future. Additionally, it is important to note that the characteristics of leachate in real landfills may differ significantly from those in simulated laboratory conditions. Seasonal variation can also influence the composition of leachate. Therefore, more pilot-scale investigations are needed to elucidate the effects of the CDs/P25 photocatalytic process on real landfill leachates.

Economic analysis of photocatalysts is essential for their widespread application. Based on our previous study [16], the manufacturing cost of CDs/P25 (0.4 dollars/g) was comparable to that of commercial P25 (0.2–3.4 dollars/g). Therefore, the CDs/P25 appears to be economically feasible for practical applications. Additionally, the photocatalytic treatment of leachate is influenced by numerous operation parameters, including leachate characteristics, catalyst dosage, light intensity, reaction time, etc [8]. This study highlights the importance of leachate characteristics on the CDs/P25 photocatalytic performance. Furthermore, factors such as catalyst dosage, light intensity, and long-term operation are expected to affect the CDs/P25 photocatalytic performance, warranting further investigation in future studies. It cannot be overlooked that the use of photocatalysts in wastewater treatment may result in residual nanoparticles remaining in the treated water, potentially causing toxic impacts on diverse living organisms and the environment [33]. For instance, Zia et al. (2023) reported that TiO2 nanoparticles could cross the blood-brain barrier and induce neurotoxicity [53]. Bagheri et al. (2018) found that CDs can cause cell shrinkage and surface perforation in eukaryotic cells [54]. Therefore, future studies should identify the environmental risks associated with CDs/P25 nanoparticles, which would facilitate their practical applications in leachate treatment.

The influence and mechanisms of leachate characteristics and microbial community on the CDs/P25 photocatalytic process were comprehensively clarified. These results implied that the CDs/P25 could effectively kill bacteria in different leachates through deforming bacterial membranes. After the CDs/P25 photocatalytic process, the percentage of dead bacteria increased to over 80.0 % in different leachates, while the bacterial gene copies decreased to over 70 % in different leachates. Moreover, the CDs/P25 could change the AHL signal and reshape the microbial communities, the Bacteroidota phylum bacteria were killed, thus inhibiting the biofilm formation. The pH and inorganic ions were the main contributors to the CDs/P25 photocatalytic performance, including bacterial disinfection and organic matter degradation. Overall, these results provided insight into the mechanism and relationships of the CDs/P25 photocatalytic performance and leachate characteristics, thereby promoting the application of the CDs/P25 photocatalytic process on practical leachate. For future studies, it is recommended to investigate the influence of CDs/P25 photocatalytic process on the biofilm characteristics. This would help promote the application of the CDs/P25 photocatalytic process on leachate.

Xu Qiyong: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Investigation, Conceptualization. Wang Qian: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Conceptualization. Wang Tong: Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation. Li Huan: Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22306006 and 22176005) and Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (2024CDJXY011).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jece.2025.116022.
